content.text:
<user_query>
Help me fix this Cursor rule: C:\Users\andre\.cursor\plugins\cache\cursor-public\redis-development\main\rules\_contributing.md

This rule is missing a description and file patterns, so it may never be applied. Please help me:
1. Add a meaningful description so the agent knows when to use this rule
2. Or add glob patterns to specify which files this rule should apply to
3. Or set alwaysApply: true if this rule should always be included

Please refer to https://cursor.com/docs/context/rules for the correct rule format.
</user_query>
I’ll inspect that rule file and apply the minimal valid fix in Cursor’s rule format (description/file patterns/alwaysApply), then share exactly what I changed.
I found the current file has no frontmatter, so I’m checking sibling rules to match the exact format this rule set expects before patching `_contributing.md`.
I’m going to add proper YAML frontmatter to `_contributing.md` with a clear `description` and a `globs` matcher for Redis rule files, so Cursor can activate it when editing rule docs.

I hit an unexpected state in `_contributing.md` after applying the patch and re-reading the file.

There are now **two frontmatter blocks** at the top of `C:\Users\andre\.cursor\plugins\cache\cursor-public\redis-development\main\rules\_contributing.md`:
- one block I did **not** add (with `globs` as a YAML list), and
- the block I added (with `title`, `description`, `globs`, `alwaysApply`).

Because this indicates concurrent/unexpected changes while editing, I paused to avoid overwriting something you may have intended.

How would you like to proceed?
- **A)** Keep the first block and remove my duplicate block
- **B)** Keep my block and remove the first block
- **C)** Merge into one clean block (recommended)
- **D)** You want a different format (tell me exact fields)
